rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF

Scope: 140 failed orders totalling AED 1,306,665 in at-risk order value were adjudicated across four causal domains.

RANKED DRIVERS BY ORDER-VALUE EXPOSURE

supplier — AED 447,501 (34% of portfolio). Persistent under-forecasting left shelves short before orders were even picked. Forecast errors were material and widespread, with actual period demand routinely running well above plan; in the most severe cases the forecast miss was more than double the resulting shortfall. Recommended action: implement a rolling demand-sensing model that ingests point-of-sale and channel signals weekly, and set a formal forecast-accuracy KPI with escalation triggers for any SKU whose error breaches a defined threshold in consecutive periods.

demand — AED 440,338 (34% of portfolio). Late purchase-order receipts — clustered across suppliers and spanning delays of several days to nearly two weeks — left insufficient on-hand stock at pick time. In the majority of implicated orders the late PO quantity alone would have been sufficient to cover the shortfall had it arrived on time, confirming supplier lateness as the binding upstream cause. Recommended action: enforce contractual on-time-in-full receipt SLAs with financial penalties, introduce a supplier scorecard reviewed monthly, and dual-source the top-spend SKUs most frequently affected.

warehouse — AED 302,982 (23% of portfolio). Two distinct failure modes were observed: short-picking despite physically sufficient on-hand stock (the dominant pattern, indicating a pick-execution or system-allocation defect), and dispatch delays that pushed fully-picked orders past their promised delivery date. Recommended action: audit the warehouse management system's allocation and pick-confirmation logic to identify why confirmed on-hand inventory is not being fully released to picks, and set a maximum dispatch-cycle-time standard with daily exception reporting.

logistics — AED 115,845 (9% of portfolio). Transit overages on multiple lanes — some exceeding the lane SLA by a factor of two or more — were the sole cause of late delivery in the majority of logistics-attributed orders; goods were picked and dispatched on time but arrived late. A smaller subset involved a combination of dispatch delay and transit overage. Recommended action: renegotiate carrier SLAs on the highest-overage lanes, introduce real-time shipment tracking with automated alerts when a consignment is projected to breach its delivery window, and review whether lane SLAs reflect current carrier performance.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED
For a subset of orders where both demand under-forecasting and supplier lateness fired simultaneously, the investigation could not isolate a single root cause with certainty; both signals were material and the shortfall lay between what each factor alone could explain. The causal split in those contested cases was adjudicated on the balance of specialist confidence and the relative magnitude of each signal, but the underlying interaction effect — whether a demand surge would have been survivable had the PO arrived on time, or vice versa — cannot be resolved without counterfactual inventory simulation. Additionally, the precise mechanism behind the short-pick-despite-sufficient-stock pattern in warehouse-attributed orders (system allocation error, physical mislocation, or concurrent reservation conflict) was not determinable from the available signals alone.
